text:
Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers a diverse range of B.Tech programs for the academic year 2025-26. Established in 1999, the Mechanical Engineering program has an intake of 120 students. The Computer Science & Engineering program, initiated in 2001, caters to 180 students annually. Electrical Engineering, launched in 2004 with an intake of 120, and Civil Engineering, introduced in 2010 with a smaller intake of 60, are also among the college's offerings.

Aeronautical Engineering, which began operations in 2013 with an intake of 60 students, focuses on the design, development, testing, and production of aircraft. The Food Technology program, established in 2019, has a modest intake of 30 students, addressing the growing demand for professionals in this field. In recent years, the college has expanded its curriculum to include cutting-edge specializations such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Data Science, which started in 2021 with an intake of 60.

Additionally, the Computer Science & Engineering program now offers a unique specialization in Internet of Things and Cyber Security Including Blockchain Technology, also launched in 2021. This innovative track prepares students for the future by integrating IoT technologies with cybersecurity principles, including blockchain technology. The latest addition to the curriculum is Robotics And Artificial Intelligence (RAI), which commenced operations in 2025 with an intake of 60 students, reflecting a forward-looking approach to engineering education that aligns with global technological trends.

Each M.Tech programme at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology aims to provide a comprehensive learning experience, blending theoretical knowledge with practical applications, making it an ideal choice for aspiring engineers seeking advanced education.

text:
For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the tuition fee structure for both First Year (FY) and Direct Second Year (DSE) admissions is clearly defined to cater to a diverse student body.

The OPEN category, which includes general students, has an annual tuition fee of Rs. 134,026 for FY and DSE admissions. This is the highest among all categories. The EBC (Economically Backward Classes), EWS (Economically Weaker Sections), and OBC (Other Backward Classes) categories also have the same fee structure at Rs. 82,505 for both FY and DSE.

The SBC (Scheduled Backward Classes), VJNT (Very Poor Economically Backward Students of Scheduled Tribes), and TFWS (Tribal Welfare Sub-Category) students are charged a lower fee of Rs. 30,983 for both FY and DSE admissions. This category aims to provide financial relief to economically disadvantaged students.

The SC (Scheduled Castes) and ST (Scheduled Tribes) categories have the lowest fees at Rs. 15,526 for both FY and DSE admissions. These reservations are provided under the affirmative action policies of the government to ensure equal educational opportunities for historically marginalized communities.

In summary, the highest fee is charged to OPEN category students at Rs. 134,026 per year, while the lowest fee is Rs. 15,526 for SC and ST students. This structured fee system ensures that all students can access quality education based on their socio-economic background.

text:
For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, a variety of hostel options are available to cater to both male and female students. The college offers four hostels: two for ladies and two for boys.

The Subhadra Ladies Hostel is specifically designed for female students and charges an annual fee of Rs. 30,000. This hostel provides a safe and comfortable living environment for outstation girls, ensuring they can focus on their studies without the worry of commuting long distances daily.

For male students, there are two options: the New Boys Hostel and the Old Boys Hostel. The New Boys Hostel is priced at Rs. 30,000 per year, while the Old Boys Hostel costs Rs. 20,000 annually. Both hostels offer accommodation for outstation boys, providing them with a supportive environment to thrive academically and socially.

The benefits of these hostels extend beyond just housing. They often include amenities such as Wi-Fi, laundry facilities, and recreational spaces. Additionally, the college ensures that all students have access to basic necessities like clean drinking water, electricity, and security measures. These facilities are crucial for outstation students who might otherwise face challenges in managing their daily needs while pursuing higher education.

In summary, Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology offers a range of affordable hostel options tailored to the needs of both male and female students from outside the local area. Each hostel not only provides a place to live but also supports the holistic development of its residents by offering a conducive environment for learning and personal growth.

Starting from Ashta, the journey begins with a monthly fee of Rs. 500, making it the most affordable option for students who live nearby. The next significant stop is Nagthane Fata, where the cost increases to Rs. 700 per month. For those residing in Amnapur or Burli, the fees rise further to Rs. 1,300 and Rs. 1,400 respectively, marking a substantial increase compared to the initial stops.

The route then continues with Kirloskarwadi, where students pay Rs. 1,900 per month for boarding. This is followed by Kundal, which charges Rs. 2,200 per month, providing an additional option for those who need more space or amenities. The costliest stop along the route is Dudhondi, with a monthly fee of Rs. 2,300, catering to students who may require premium accommodation and services.

This diverse range of boarding options ensures that commuting students from various financial backgrounds can find suitable accommodations without compromising on their educational commitments. The Ashta-Dudhondi route serves a wide array of students, offering flexibility in terms of location and cost, thereby making it an essential part of the college's support infrastructure for its student body.

The Ashta-Karad route is a popular choice among commuting students at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta. This route offers a variety of accommodation options with varying costs per month, catering to the diverse needs of students.

This route provides a balance between proximity to home and affordability, making it an attractive option for students who wish to minimize their commuting expenses while still being close enough to the college campus.

text:
In the context of Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology for the academic year 2025-26, the cutoffs for the OPEN category General seats across various engineering branches provide a clear picture of their competitiveness. The Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) branch stands out as one of the most competitive with an impressive cutoff score of 87.14 marks. This high demand is likely due to its relevance in today's digital age, making it an attractive choice for students aiming for placements in tech companies.

On the other hand, the Food Technology and CSE IOT branches also have relatively high cutoffs at 89.54 and 83.07 marks respectively, indicating a strong competition for these specialized fields as well. These branches require a robust understanding of both engineering principles and their application in specific industries.

Among the more accessible options are Mechanical Engineering (Mech) with a cutoff of 76.93 marks, Aerospace Engineering (AERO) at 76.27 marks, and Electrical Engineering (ELECT) with a score of 77.95 marks. These branches still require a solid academic performance but may offer more flexibility in terms of career opportunities.

Civil Engineering (CIVIL), with its cutoff of 73.63 marks, is another accessible option, making it easier for students to secure admission compared to the highly competitive branches mentioned earlier. Additionally, the Railway Airsignalling and Interlocking (RAI) branch has a slightly higher cutoff at 79.72 marks but remains more approachable than CSE or Food Technology.

For students looking for a balance between competitiveness and accessibility, Aerospace Engineering and Civil Engineering are good choices. These branches offer a reasonable entry point while still providing valuable skills in their respective fields. Overall, the choice of branch should be based on individual interests and career aspirations, with careful consideration of the cutoff scores provided.

text:
The MHT-CET cutoff for the Mechanical Engineering branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta for the academic year 2025-26 varies significantly across different categories and test series. The cutoffs are as follows:

**VJ (Very Jai) Category:**
For General candidates, the cutoff is set at 50.70 marks, while for Ladies, it is slightly lower at 44.26 marks. This suggests that the college has a more lenient admission criteria for female students in this category.

**NT-1 (National Test - I) Category:**
The General candidates face a higher hurdle with a cutoff of 71.66 marks, whereas the Ladies category requires only 40.68 marks. This indicates that there is a significant difference between the requirements for male and female students in this test series.

**NT-2 (National Test - II) Category:**
The General candidates must secure at least 71.48 marks to qualify, which is slightly lower than NT-1 but still higher than VJ category. For Ladies, the cutoff is significantly higher at 70.62 marks, indicating a more stringent requirement for female students.

**NT-3 (National Test - III) Category:**
The General candidates need only 25.21 marks to qualify, which is notably lower compared to other categories. However, there are no specific cutoffs provided for the Ladies category, suggesting that this test series may not be as competitive or relevant for female applicants.

**OBC (Other Backward Classes) Category:**
The General candidates in this category need a minimum of 65.31 marks, while the Ladies require 68.47 marks. This shows a slight preference towards female students within the OBC category.

**SEBC (Specially Educated Backward Class) Category:**
For General candidates, the cutoff is set at 75.40 marks, which is higher than the General category but lower for Ladies at 73.13 marks. This suggests a more favorable admission process for female SEBC students.

**OPEN (General) Category:**
The General candidates must secure 76.93 marks to qualify, while the Ladies need 75.40 marks. This indicates that even within the general category, there is a slight preference towards female applicants.

**SC (Scheduled Caste) Category:**
For General candidates, the cutoff is set at 40.68 marks for both General and Ladies categories, which is significantly lower than other categories. This suggests a more relaxed admission criteria for SC students in this test series.

**ST (Scheduled Tribe) Category:**
There are no specific cutoffs provided for either General or Ladies categories, indicating that candidates from the ST category may not be required to meet any MHT-CET score for admission through this test series.

**DEF (Defence) Category:**
The General candidates need 52.36 marks, while there are no specific requirements mentioned for Ladies. This suggests a more relaxed admission process for female Defence students compared to other categories.

**TFWS (Tribal Welfare Scheme) Category:**
For General candidates, the cutoff is set at 83.11 marks, which is significantly higher than most other categories. There are no specific requirements mentioned for Ladies in this category.

**EWS (Economically Weaker Sections) Category:**
The General candidates need only 28.98 marks to qualify, while there are no specific requirements mentioned for Ladies. This suggests a more relaxed admission process for EWS students compared to other categories.

These cutoffs reflect the diverse and inclusive admission policies of Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, with varying levels of competitiveness across different categories and test series.

text:
The MHT-CET cutoff scores for the Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta for the academic year 2025-26 provide a detailed insight into the admission criteria across various categories. These cutoffs are essential for understanding the minimum marks required to secure a seat in the CSE program.

**VJ Category:**
The VJ category, which stands for Very Jat (a subcategory of General), has different cutoff scores for male and female candidates. The general category requires 75.21 marks, while the ladies category demands 78.34 marks. This indicates that female applicants in this category have a slight advantage over their male counterparts.

**NT-1/2/3 Categories:**
The NT (Non-Technical) categories are further divided into NT-1, NT-2, and NT-3, each with distinct cutoffs for general and ladies candidates. For the general category, NT-1 requires 83.34 marks, followed by NT-2 at 86.68 marks, and NT-3 at 77.01 marks. The ladies category sees higher cutoffs across all subcategories: NT-1 at 87.93 marks, NT-2 at 88.19 marks, and NT-3 at 79.91 marks. This suggests that the competition is more intense for female candidates in these categories.

**OBC Category (Other Backward Classes):**
The OBC category has a general cutoff of 84.33 marks and a ladies cutoff of 88.56 marks. The difference between the two categories highlights the preference given to female applicants within this category, as they need to score higher than their male counterparts.

**SEBC Category (Specifically Educated Backward Classes):**
The SEBC category has slightly higher cutoffs compared to OBC for both general and ladies candidates. General candidates require 86.20 marks, while ladies need 90.87 marks. This indicates a significant preference for female applicants in this category.

**OPEN Category:**
For the OPEN (General) category, the cutoff is set at 87.14 marks for general candidates and 87.41 marks for ladies. The slight difference between these two categories suggests that while there are no reservations, women still have a higher threshold to meet compared to men.

**SC Category (Scheduled Castes):**
The SC category has the lowest cutoffs among all categories. General candidates need only 70.50 marks, and ladies require 73.75 marks. This indicates that the college is making significant efforts to ensure representation from this community by lowering the bar for admission.

**ST Category (Scheduled Tribes):**
The ST category has even lower cutoffs than SC. General candidates need just 63.65 marks, and there are no specific cutoffs provided for ladies in this category, indicating that the college is prioritizing admission of tribal students without additional barriers based on gender.

**DEF Category (Defence Personnel):**
For DEF (Defence Personnel) candidates, the general category requires 81.94 marks, with no specific cutoff mentioned for ladies. This suggests a focus on merit but also acknowledges the contribution and service of defence personnel by setting a relatively high threshold.

**TFWS Category (Tribal Welfare Scheme):**
The TFWS category has the highest cutoffs among all categories, particularly for general candidates at 90.43 marks, with no specific cutoff provided for ladies. This indicates that while there is significant reservation in this category, the merit-based criteria are stringent.

**EWS Category (Economically Weaker Sections):**
The EWS category has a general cutoff of 71.56 marks and no specific cutoff mentioned for ladies. This suggests an effort to include economically weaker sections without imposing additional gender-specific requirements.

In summary, these MHT-CET cutoffs reflect the college's commitment to diverse representation while maintaining high standards of merit. The differences in scores between categories and genders highlight both affirmative action policies and a focus on academic excellence across various social groups.

This suggests that despite fewer placements, companies were still interested and offered more opportunities to the Food Technology graduates.

These trends could be indicative of several factors, including market conditions, company strategies, or even internal changes in the college's placement process. text:
For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the placement scenario across various engineering branches showcases a diverse range of opportunities for students. Mechanical Engineering leads with an impressive 96 placements out of 122 offers, averaging 5 LPA (Lakhs Per Annum), with the highest package reaching up to 8.5 LPA. This branch not only boasts the largest number of placements but also stands out with its top package.

Civil Engineering follows closely with 34 students securing 37 offers at an average salary of 5 LPA, with a peak offer of 6.5 LPA. The Electrical Engineering department sees 153 students receiving 171 offers, averaging 5 LPA and offering the highest package of 6.5 LPA. This branch is notable for its high number of placements despite having fewer students compared to Mechanical.

Computer Science Engineering (CSE) has a robust placement record with 145 students getting 120 offers, achieving an average salary of 5.2 LPA and the highest package of 12.34 LPA. This branch significantly outperforms others in terms of both offer count and top package, making it one of the most sought-after branches.

Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering has a slightly lower number of students with 69 participants securing 41 offers at an average salary of 5.2 LPA, with the highest package being 8.67 LPA. This branch is notable for its focus on emerging technologies and cybersecurity, which are in high demand.

Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering (AI & DS) has 73 students receiving 43 offers at an average salary of 5.2 LPA, with the highest package being 7.5 LPA. This branch is gaining traction due to its relevance in today’s data-driven world but currently lags behind CSE and Electrical Engineering in terms of both offer count and top packages.

Aeronautical Engineering has a smaller student body with 48 participants securing 33 offers, averaging 4.6 LPA, with the highest package at 6 LPA. This branch is less competitive compared to others but still provides valuable opportunities for students interested in aerospace technology.

Food Technology sees 8 students receiving 21 offers, averaging 4 LPA and offering a top package of 5 LPA. While this branch has fewer placements, it remains an important area with growing relevance in the food industry.

In summary, Computer Science Engineering stands out as the top-performing branch, both in terms of offer count (120) and highest package (12.34 LPA). Mechanical Engineering comes second with 96 placements and a high average salary of 5 LPA, while Civil Engineering and Electrical Engineering are close behind, offering competitive packages despite having fewer students.

The college has managed to secure offers from 105 different companies for the 588 placements made in the academic year 2025-26. This diversity in companies ensures that students have a wide range of opportunities across various sectors, making Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology a preferred choice for engineering aspirants.

text:
For the academic year 2024-25 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the placement scenario across various branches is quite diverse, showcasing a range of opportunities for students.

In Mechanical Engineering, 87 students secured placements with an average package of 4.7 LPA (Lakhs Per Annum), with the highest package reaching up to 6.5 LPA. This branch not only boasts a significant number of offers but also provides a competitive environment where students can secure lucrative packages.

Civil Engineering, with 70 students and 30 placements, has an average package of 4.8 LPA, with the highest being 7.5 LPA. Despite having fewer placements compared to Mechanical Engineering, Civil Engineering still manages to offer some impressive packages, highlighting its importance in the industry.

Electrical Engineering stands out as one of the most sought-after branches, with 146 students and 127 offers, averaging a package of 5.2 LPA, with the highest package reaching up to 9 LPA. This branch's high offer count and top-tier packages make it particularly attractive for students.

Computer Science Engineering (CSE) has 131 students and 90 placements, offering an average package of 5.2 LPA, with the highest being 8 LPA. CSE is a popular choice due to its relevance in today's tech-driven world, but despite having fewer offers than Electrical Engineering, it still provides strong packages.

The addition of Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering introduces another dimension to the placement scenario. With 62 students and 40 placements, this branch also averages an impressive package of 5.2 LPA, with the highest being 7.5 LPA. This specialization in emerging technologies is a significant draw for many students.

Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering (AI & DS) has 70 students and 47 placements, averaging 5.2 LPA, with the highest package at 6 LPA. This branch's focus on cutting-edge technology makes it highly competitive, offering strong packages despite a slightly lower number of offers.

Aeronautical Engineering is another specialized field with 33 students and 28 placements, averaging 4.6 LPA, with the highest being 4.5 LPA. While this branch has fewer students and placements compared to others, it still provides competitive packages.

Food Technology, with only 21 students and 17 placements, averages a package of 3.5 LPA, with the highest at 4 LPA. Although smaller in scale, Food Technology still manages to provide opportunities for its students.

Overall, Electrical Engineering emerges as the top branch by both offer count (127) and average package (5.2 LPA), making it a standout choice for students seeking high-paying placements. However, branches like Mechanical Engineering and Civil Engineering also offer strong packages with fewer placements, providing a mix of opportunities across various engineering disciplines.

The college's placement record shows that 90+ companies have extended offers to the students, totaling 511 placements so far. This diverse range of companies from both public and private sectors ensures a wide array of career options for the students.

text:
For the academic year 2023-24 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the placement scenario across various branches has been quite diverse. Mechanical Engineering led with a strong performance, offering 105 placements to 112 students, with an average package of 4.5 LPA and a highest package of 33 LPA. This branch demonstrated robust demand from employers, reflecting its practical applicability in the industry.

Civil Engineering followed closely behind, with 99 offers out of 73 students, again averaging 4.5 LPA but with the highest package at only 6 LPA. The lower average and highest package suggest that while there is a good number of placements, the opportunities might be more evenly distributed compared to Mechanical Engineering.

Electrical Engineering saw an impressive placement count of 117 out of 145 students, averaging 4.8 LPA with a top offer of 9 LPA. This branch's higher average package indicates that it is highly sought after by employers, likely due to the growing demand for electrical engineers in various sectors.

Computer Science Engineering had 142 placements out of 153 students, offering an average salary of 5 LPA with a highest package of 10 LPA. This branch's strong performance reflects its relevance and high demand, making it one of the top choices among students for future career prospects.

Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering did not have any placements or offers reported this year, which is noteworthy given the increasing importance of these fields in today’s tech-driven world. The lack of data suggests that either there were no suitable candidates or the field was less popular among students compared to other branches.

Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering also had no reported placements or offers, indicating a similar situation as AI and Data Science Engineering. This could be due to various factors such as student preferences, course content, or market demand at that time.

Aeronautical Engineering saw 55 out of 55 students placed with an average package of 4.3 LPA and the highest being 5.5 LPA. This branch's performance is commendable given the specific nature of its curriculum, but it still lags behind some other branches in terms of both placement numbers and average packages.

Food Technology had a slightly lower number of placements with 36 out of 40 students placed, averaging 3.8 LPA with a highest package of 4.5 LPA. This branch's performance is solid but shows room for improvement compared to the top-performing branches like Mechanical Engineering and Computer Science Engineering.

In summary, Mechanical Engineering emerged as the standout branch this year, boasting both the highest number of placements (105) and the highest individual package (33 LPA). This success can be attributed to its practical applications and strong industry demand. Other branches such as Electrical Engineering and Computer Science Engineering also performed well with high placement numbers and competitive packages. However, it is important for the college to address the lack of placements in AI and Data Science Engineering and Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering to ensure a balanced growth across all engineering disciplines.

text:
For admission to Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in the academic year 2025-26, students applying for B.Tech., BBA, or BCA programs must submit a comprehensive set of documents. Here is an explanation of each required document:

1. **MHT-CET or JEE 2026 Marksheet**: This document verifies your performance in the entrance examination, which is crucial for determining eligibility and ranking.

2. **10th Marksheet**: It confirms your secondary school education completion and academic background, essential for verifying educational qualifications.

3. **12th Marksheet**: This document provides details of your higher secondary education, necessary for assessing your academic performance and suitability for engineering or business studies.

4. **Diploma Marksheet (if applicable)**: If you are applying with a diploma, this marksheet is required to validate your technical background.

5. **Leaving / Transfer Certificate**: This document confirms that you have completed your education at the previous institution, ensuring there are no gaps in your academic history.

6. **AADHAR Card (with linked mobile number)**: AADHAR serves as a unique identification and is required for various administrative purposes, including financial transactions and scholarship verification.

7. **Migration Certificate**: This document is necessary if you have moved from another state or country to pursue higher education in Maharashtra.

8. **Caste Certificate**: It verifies your caste category, which is important for determining eligibility under reservation policies.

9. **Caste / Tribe Validity Certificate (valid upto 31 March 2027)**: This certificate ensures that the caste/tribe status remains valid during the academic year, impacting scholarship and reservation benefits.

10. **Non-Creamy Layer Certificate**: This document certifies that you belong to a non-creamy layer category, which is relevant for certain scholarships and reservations.

11. **Income Certificate for 2025-26**: It helps in assessing your financial background, which can influence the eligibility for various scholarships.

12. **Orphan Candidate Document (if applicable)**: This document verifies that you are an orphan, potentially qualifying you for specific scholarships or benefits.

13. **Disability Certificate (Online) from District Civil Surgeon (for physically challenged students)**: This certificate is essential to ensure that students with disabilities receive appropriate support and accommodations.

14. **Defence Certificate**: Beneficiaries of the Defence Services can apply for certain reservations and scholarships, and this document verifies eligibility.

15. **Domicile / Nationality Certificate (Father or Mother)**: This document confirms your domicile status in Maharashtra or your nationality, which is important for determining reservation categories.

16. **Minority Certificate**: Required if you belong to a minority community, it ensures that you are eligible for reserved seats and scholarships under the Minority Reservation policy.

17. **अल्पभूधारक दाखिला (तहसिलदार)**: This document is specific to students from economically weaker sections and confirms their eligibility for EWS reservations.

The requirements do not differ significantly by reservation category, but the submission of relevant certificates such as Caste Certificate, Non-Creamy Layer Certificate, Minority Certificate, and Domicile / Nationality Certificate (Father or Mother) may vary based on your specific background. Always ensure that all documents are up-to-date and accurately reflect your status to avoid any discrepancies during the admission process.

1. **MHT-CET or JEE 2026 Marksheet**: This document verifies your performance in the entrance examination, which is crucial for determining eligibility and ranking.

2. **10th Marksheet**: It confirms your secondary school education completion and academic background, essential for verifying educational qualifications.

3. **12th Marksheet**: This document provides details of your higher secondary education, necessary for assessing your academic performance and suitability for engineering or business studies.

4. **Diploma Marksheet (if applicable)**: If you are applying with a diploma, this marksheet is required to validate your technical background.

5. **Leaving / Transfer Certificate**: This document confirms that you have completed your education at the previous institution, ensuring there are no gaps in your academic history.

6. **AADHAR Card (with linked mobile number)**: AADHAR serves as a unique identification and is required for various administrative purposes, including financial transactions and scholarship verification.

7. **Migration Certificate**: This document is necessary if you have moved from another state or country to pursue higher education in Maharashtra.

8. **Caste Certificate**: It verifies your caste category, which is important for determining eligibility under reservation policies.

9. **Caste / Tribe Validity Certificate (valid upto 31 March 2027)**: This certificate ensures that the caste/tribe status remains valid during the academic year, impacting scholarship and reservation benefits.

10. **Non-Creamy Layer Certificate**: This document certifies that you belong to a non-creamy layer category, which is relevant for certain scholarships and reservations.

11. **Income Certificate for 2025-26**: It helps in assessing your financial background, which can influence the eligibility for various scholarships.

12. **Orphan Candidate Document (if applicable)**: This document verifies that you are an orphan, potentially qualifying you for specific scholarships or benefits.

13. **Disability Certificate (Online) from District Civil Surgeon (for physically challenged students)**: This certificate is essential to ensure that students with disabilities receive appropriate support and accommodations.

14. **Defence Certificate**: Beneficiaries of the Defence Services can apply for certain reservations and scholarships, and this document verifies eligibility.

15. **Domicile / Nationality Certificate (Father or Mother)**: This document confirms your domicile status in Maharashtra or your nationality, which is important for determining reservation categories.

16. **Minority Certificate**: Required if you belong to a minority community, it ensures that you are eligible for reserved seats and scholarships under the Minority Reservation policy.

17. **अल्पभूधारक दाखिला (तहसिलदार)**: This document is specific to students from economically weaker sections and confirms their eligibility for EWS reservations.

The requirements do not differ significantly by reservation category, but the submission of relevant certificates such as Caste Certificate, Non-Creamy Layer Certificate, Minority Certificate, and Domicile / Nationality Certificate (Father or Mother) may vary based on your specific background. Always ensure that all documents are up-to-date and accurately reflect your status to avoid any discrepancies during the admission process.

text:
For admission to Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology, Ashta for the academic year 2025-26 under the DSY (Diploma-Specialized Year) program, students are required to submit a comprehensive set of documents. Each document serves a specific purpose in verifying eligibility, background, and other relevant details.

1. **10th Marksheet**: This is essential for establishing the educational background of the candidate from secondary school. It helps verify the academic history before proceeding with higher education.
2. **12th Marksheet**: Similar to the 10th marksheet, this document verifies the candidate's performance in high school and is crucial for admission into engineering programs.
3. **Diploma Marksheet**: This document provides details of the diploma holder’s academic performance, which is necessary as many DSY programs are designed for students who have completed a diploma course.
4. **Leaving / Transfer Certificate**: This certificate ensures that there are no pending issues with the candidate's current or previous educational institution and helps in smooth transition to the new college.
5. **Domicile / Nationality Certificate**: This document verifies the candidate’s residential status, which is important for determining eligibility under various reservation categories.
6. **AADHAR Card (with linked mobile number)**: The AADHAR card serves as a unique identification and helps in verifying the candidate's identity and ensures that all information provided by the candidate matches official records.
7. **Caste Certificate**: This document is required to verify the caste or tribe of the candidate, which is crucial for determining eligibility under reservation policies based on social categories.
8. **Caste / Tribe Validity Certificate**: This certificate confirms the validity period of the caste/tribe status, ensuring that it remains applicable during the admission process and subsequent years.
9. **Non-Creamy Layer Certificate (valid up to 31 March 2027)**: This document certifies that the candidate belongs to a non-creamy layer category, which is important for certain reservations and scholarships.
10. **Income Certificate for 2025-26**: This certificate verifies the financial status of the candidate or their family, helping in determining eligibility for various scholarships and aid programs.
11. **Orphan Candidate Document**: If applicable, this document certifies that the candidate is an orphan, which can be a criterion for certain scholarships and reservations.
12. **Disability Certificate (Online) from District Civil Surgeon**: This certificate verifies any physical or mental disability of the candidate, which may qualify them for special accommodations and support services.
13. **Defence Certificate**: This document certifies that the candidate is a child of a serving or retired defense personnel, potentially qualifying them for certain reservations and benefits.
14. **Domicile / Nationality Certificate (Father or Mother)**: Similar to the main Domicile/Nationality certificate, this verifies the domicile status through either parent, which can be important in cases where the candidate’s own document is not available.

The requirements do differ slightly by reservation category. For instance, certain certificates like the Caste Certificate and Non-Creamy Layer Certificate are mandatory for all categories but may have different validity periods or additional verification steps depending on the specific category. Similarly, documents related to disabilities or defense personnel status might be required only if applicable to the candidate's background.

It is crucial that candidates ensure they submit all these documents accurately and in time to avoid any delays in their admission process.
